Menstrual hygiene · Tanzania

Keeping girls in school, with dignity.

Many girls from low-income families in Tanzania miss school every month simply because they lack menstrual products. The Binti Project changes that — with a reusable hygiene kit and education that lets girls manage their period safely, confidently and sustainably.

Swahili wisdom — “Education is the key to life”
The challenge

Why menstrual hygiene keeps girls out of school

Menstrual hygiene is a basic part of health, dignity and self-determination — a right every woman should have access to. Yet for many girls from low-income families in Tanzania, products are simply unavailable or unaffordable. The result: recurring absences that hold back their education and their future.

Menstruation is a natural process, but it is still surrounded by shame, taboo and a lack of education. Combined with poverty and missing infrastructure, this deepens the disadvantage girls already face.

1–3school days a girl loses every single month during her period
  • ✕Missed lessons. Many girls skip school during their period; in some regions they are excluded from daily life altogether.
  • ✕Unhygienic substitutes. Unable to afford clean pads, girls resort to old rags or banana fibres.
  • ✕Nothing available. In rural areas especially, affordable hygiene products are almost impossible to find.
  • ✕Disposables don't work. Single-use pads are no sustainable solution for poor families — and harm the environment when not disposed of properly.
2 yrs

Fund a Binti Kit
Our impact

Measurable change, girl by girl

A 2015 report by the Tanzania Water and Sanitation Network (TAWASANET) found that most schoolgirls miss one to three days of lessons every month — largely because of inadequate sanitation facilities.

By distributing Binti Kits alongside awareness sessions, AfriCraft helps break down these structural barriers and keep girls in the education system for the long term.

1–3
school days missed per girl, monthly
63%
of school toilets lack pad disposal
38%
of schools lack adequate water/washing
Mama Lishe project

Extending our reach beyond schools

A collaboration between AfriCraft, Emina Company, EBf Company and GIZ, raising awareness of menstrual hygiene and cleanliness among women in the informal sector — food vendors (“Mama Lishe”), fish sellers, bus conductors and drivers across Dar es Salaam.

6,100

reusable pads provided

1,000

aprons distributed

300

hand-wash units set up

About AfriCraft

A fair-trade social enterprise, since 2004

AfriCraft empowers communities and young people through waste collection and recycling, promoting sustainable living and fair trade across Tanzania. What began as a small home workshop with two employees now works with over 200 artisans.

The same artisans who turn discarded materials into handcrafted products also produce the Binti Kits — connecting social impact with environmental responsibility. AfriCraft follows World Fair Trade Organization standards and pays fair wages.

Refuse · Reduce · Reuse · Recycle Conservation · Culture · Community · Commerce WFTO fair trade
Your donation at work

Three ways your gift keeps girls learning

Fund a Binti Kit

Everything one girl needs to manage her period with dignity — reusable pads, waterproof pouches, soap, underwear and a guide — for up to two years.

Fund education

Awareness sessions for girls and boys that break the taboo around menstruation and turn stigma into everyday understanding.

Partner with us

Schools, companies and NGOs can fund kits at scale and bring menstrual dignity to whole communities. Let's build it together.

Support the Binti Project

Your donation keeps a girl in school

As a non-profit, AfriCraft commits every donation solely to its charitable goals — strengthening communities, supporting young people, protecting the environment and driving sustainable development. Our focus is impact, not profit.
